text:
```md AI-PM-Tool/project_requirements.md # AI-Driven Project Management Tool: Project Requirements Document ## 1. Overview This document outlines the high-level requirements for an **AI-driven project management (PM) tool**. The solution will automate task creation from a project description, intelligently assign tasks to team members based on workload, and offer a minimal yet functional Kanban interface for tracking progress. --- ## 2. Project Scope - **Goal**: Reduce overhead in project management by leveraging AI to: 1. Automatically generate requirements/tasks from a written project description. 2. Assign those tasks to the most suitable team members via a basic load-balancing algorithm. 3. Provide a simple Kanban board for task visualization. - **Primary Users**: - **Admin**: Creates new projects, can invite or remove team members. - **Regular User**: Can view and work on assigned tasks; can create tasks manually if needed. - **Core Deliverables**: 1. **User Authentication** (sign up, login, logout). 2. **Project Management** (creation, membership). 3. **Automatic Task Generation** (AI-based). 4. **Load Balancer** for Task Assignment. 5. **Task Tracking UI** (Kanban or list view). --- ## 3. Functional Requirements ### 3.1 User & Authentication 1. **User Registration** - Users register with email, password, and (optionally) name. - System stores credentials securely (hashed password). 2. **Login & Logout** - Users can log in to access their projects and tasks. - Basic JWT (JSON Web Token) or session-based auth for protected routes. 3. **Roles** - **Admin**: Can create projects, invite new members. - **Regular User**: Can manage tasks within projects they belong to. ### 3.2 Projects 1. **Create Project** - Admin enters project name, description, chooses whether to auto-generate tasks or not. 2. **Invite Members** - Admin can invite users by email or username. 3. **View Projects** - User sees a list of projects they belong to. 4. **Project Details** - Clicking a project displays the task board (Kanban) and relevant info. ### 3.3 Automatic Task Generation (AI) 1. **Project Description Input** - When creating a project (or at a separate step), Admin provides a high-level description (e.g., "An e-commerce site with payment integration..."). 2. **AI Processing** - The system calls an **LLM endpoint** or integrated model, prompting it to break the description into tasks. - Each task contains: - **Title** - **Short Description** - **Estimated Difficulty** (e.g., a 1–10 scale or "Easy/Med/Hard") 3. **Review & Confirm** - The user sees the proposed tasks, can edit or remove them. - Finalizes the list to create real tasks in the database. 4. **Auto-Assignment** - The load balancer assigns these new tasks to team members based on current workload or skill (MVP can be "lowest In Progress tasks = highest priority to receive a new task"). ### 3.4 Manual Task Management 1. **Create a Task** - Title (required), description (optional), difficulty (optional). - The system **suggests** an assignee (load balancer). 2. **Task Status** - **To Do**, **In Progress**, **Done** (minimal set for MVP). - User can change status or reassign as needed. 3. **Task Editing & Deletion** - Edit title, description, assignee, etc. - Admin or task creator can delete if not needed. ### 3.5 Load Balancer 1. **Algorithm** - Maintains a basic count of how many tasks each user has *In Progress*. - On task creation (manual or auto-generated), assign to the user with the fewest tasks in progress. - (Optional) Factor in skill ratings if time permits. 2. **Overriding** - Manual overrides allowed: users or Admins can reassign tasks if needed. ### 3.6 Kanban Board (UI) 1. **Board Columns** - **To Do**, **In Progress**, **Done**. 2. **Task Cards** - Show title, assignee, difficulty, and a quick edit option. 3. **Drag & Drop** (Optional MVP Feature) - If time permits; otherwise, a dropdown or simple status change button is sufficient. 4. **Filtering** - Optionally filter tasks by status or assignee. ### 3.7 Notifications (Optional) - **Assign/Transfer**: Send email or Slack notification when a task is assigned or reassigned. - **Daily Summary**: A single email with tasks in progress, due soon, etc. --- ## 4. Non-Functional Requirements ### 4.1 Performance - The system must handle basic CRUD operations without significant lag for small to medium-sized teams (e.g., up to 50 users, a few hundred tasks). - AI calls (task generation) can be asynchronous or queued to avoid blocking the main UI if model inference is slow. ### 4.2 Security - Passwords stored with secure hashing (e.g., bcrypt or Argon2). - All API endpoints requiring authentication must validate JWT/session tokens. - If dealing with sensitive data, consider HTTPS (SSL/TLS) by default. ### 4.3 Maintainability - Codebase organized into clear modules (auth, projects, tasks, AI). - Documentation for the AI prompt(s) and how tasks are generated. ### 4.4 Scalability (Future Consideration) - Potential to move AI logic into a dedicated microservice if usage grows. - Database indexing for tasks if queries become complex at scale. --- ## 5. Technical Overview ### 5.1 Backend (Python + FastAPI) 1. **Core Endpoints** - `/auth/register`, `/auth/login` - `/projects` (CRUD) - `/tasks` (CRUD) - `/ai/generate-tasks` (handles project description → task suggestions) 2. **Load Balancer Logic** - Middleware or service function that checks current `InProgressTaskCount` per user. 3. **AI Integration** - Lightweight LLM or Hugging Face Transformers in Python. - Prompt engineering to parse high-level descriptions into task lists. ### 5.2 Frontend (Next.js + React) 1. **Pages** - **Login/Register** - **Project List** (dashboard) - **Project Detail / Board** - **Task Creation/Editing** - **AI Generation** (text input for project spec, then review tasks) 2. **UI Components** - KanbanBoard, TaskCard, etc. 3. **API Calls** - Use `fetch` or `axios` to communicate with FastAPI endpoints. ### 5.3 Database (PostgreSQL) - **users** - `id, email, password_hash, role, created_at` - **projects** - `id, name, description, owner_id, created_at` - **project_members** - `user_id, project_id, role` - **tasks** - `id, project_id, title, description, status, assignee_id, difficulty, created_at, updated_at` --- ## 6. System Flow 1. **User Logs In** - Gets token or session. Sees list of their projects. 2. **Create Project** - Option to auto-generate tasks by providing a description. - AI service returns suggested tasks → user confirms → tasks get created and assigned. 3. **View & Manage Tasks** - Kanban board or task list for each project. - Change status, edit details, or reassign to someone else. 4. **Load Balancing** - Whenever new tasks are created, the system finds the user with the lowest active load and assigns automatically. 5. **(Optional) Notifications** - Task assignment triggers an email/slack message. --- ## 7. Timeline & Milestones 1. **Milestone 1**: Auth + Basic Project/Task CRUD 2. **Milestone 2**: AI Task Generation & Load Balancing Integration 3. **Milestone 3**: Frontend Kanban Board & UI Polish 4. **Milestone 4**: Optional Notifications, Testing & Bug Fixes 5. **Release MVP**: Deploy to a staging/production environment (Docker, Heroku, Render, etc.) --- ## 8. Acceptance Criteria - **Users** can authenticate and see only their own projects/tasks. - **AI** can parse at least a basic project description (1–2 paragraphs) into multiple tasks with difficulty estimates. - **Load Balancer** successfully distributes tasks among multiple project members without manual intervention. - **UI** provides a Kanban or list-style view to track tasks in different statuses. - **Minimal Overhead**: The tool should reduce manual task creation rather than increase complexity. --- ### End of Requirements ``` Lets start making this project. Where should we start? To start this project, we should follow a structured approach to ensure that we build a solid foundation and progressively add features. Here's a step-by-step plan to get started: ### Step 1: Set Up the Development Environment 1. **Version Control**: Initialize a Git repository to manage your codebase. 2. **Backend Setup**: - Install Python and FastAPI. - Set up a virtual environment. - Install necessary dependencies (FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, bcrypt, etc.). 3. **Frontend Setup**: - Install Node.js and npm. - Set up a Next.js project. 4. **Database Setup**: - Install PostgreSQL. - Create a new database for the project. ### Step 2: Implement User Authentication 1. **Backend**: - Create the FastAPI project structure. - Implement user registration and login endpoints. - Set up JWT authentication. - Create database models for users. - Implement password hashing and secure storage. 2. **Frontend**: - Create pages for user registration and login. - Implement forms and handle API calls to the backend. - Store JWT tokens securely (e.g., in HttpOnly cookies or local storage). ### Step 3: Basic Project and Task Management 1. **Backend**: - Create database models for projects and tasks. - Implement CRUD endpoints for projects and tasks. - Set up relationships between users, projects, and tasks. 2. **Frontend**: - Create a dashboard to list user projects. - Implement project creation and task management interfaces. - Handle API calls to create, read, update, and delete projects and tasks. ### Step 4: AI Task Generation 1. **Backend**: - Integrate an AI model (e.g., using Hugging Face Transformers) to generate tasks from project descriptions. - Implement an endpoint to handle task generation requests. 2. **Frontend**: - Create an interface for admins to input project descriptions. - Display generated tasks for review and confirmation. ### Step 5: Load Balancer for Task Assignment 1. **Backend**: - Implement a basic load-balancing algorithm to assign tasks based on user workload. - Integrate this logic into task creation endpoints. 2. **Frontend**: - Ensure that tasks are displayed with their assigned users. - Allow manual reassignment of tasks if needed. ### Step 6: Kanban Board UI 1. **Frontend**: - Implement a Kanban board to visualize tasks. - Allow drag-and-drop functionality for changing task status (if time permits). - Provide filtering options for tasks by status or assignee. ### Step 7: Optional Features and Polishing 1. **Notifications**: - Implement email or Slack notifications for task assignments and daily summaries. 2. **Testing and Bug Fixes**: - Write unit and integration tests for both backend and frontend. - Perform thorough testing to identify and fix bugs. ### Step 8: Deployment 1. **Containerization**: - Use Docker to containerize the application. - Create Dockerfiles for both backend and frontend. 2. **Deployment**: - Deploy the application to a cloud platform (e.g., Heroku, Render, AWS). - Set up a CI/CD pipeline for automated deployments. ### Step 9: Documentation 1. **Code Documentation**: - Document the codebase, including API endpoints and important functions. - Provide clear instructions for setting up and running the project locally. 2. **User Documentation**: - Create a user guide for admins and regular users. - Include instructions for common tasks like creating projects, generating tasks, and using the Kanban board. By following this structured approach, we can ensure that the project is built systematically and efficiently. Let's start with Step 1: Setting up the development environment. What all dependencies do I need? To set up the development environment for both the backend (FastAPI) and frontend (Next.js), you'll need to install several dependencies. Below is a list of the essential dependencies for each part of the project. ### Backend (FastAPI) 1. **FastAPI**: The web framework for building APIs. 2. **Uvicorn**: ASGI server for running FastAPI. 3. **SQLAlchemy**: ORM for database interactions. 4. **Alembic**: Database migrations tool. 5. **Pydantic**: Data validation and settings management. 6. **bcrypt**: Password hashing. 7. **JWT (PyJWT)**: JSON Web Token for authentication. 8. **HTTPX**: For making HTTP requests (if needed for AI integration). 9. **Hugging Face Transformers**: For AI task generation (if using a pre-trained model). 10. **Psycopg2**: PostgreSQL adapter for Python. ### Frontend (Next.js) 1. **Next.js**: React framework for server-side rendering. 2. **React**: Library for building user interfaces. 3. **Axios**: For making HTTP requests to the backend. 4. **React DnD**: For drag-and-drop functionality in the Kanban board. 5. **Tailwind CSS**: For styling (optional, you can choose any CSS framework or plain CSS). 6. **Formik**: For form handling. 7. **Yup**: For form validation. ### Installation Commands #### Backend (FastAPI) First, create a virtual environment and activate it: ```bash python -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate # On Windows use `venv\Scripts\activate` ``` Then, install the dependencies: ```bash pip install fastapi uvicorn sqlalchemy alembic pydantic bcrypt pyjwt httpx psycopg2-binary pip install transformers # If using Hugging Face Transformers for AI ``` #### Frontend (Next.js) First, create a new Next.js project: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest ai-pm-tool-frontend cd ai-pm-tool-frontend ``` Then, install the dependencies: ```bash npm install axios react-dnd react-dnd-html5-backend tailwindcss formik yup ``` ### Additional Setup #### Backend 1. **Create a `.env` file** for environment variables (e.g., database URL, JWT secret). 2. **Initialize Alembic** for database migrations: ```bash alembic init alembic ``` 3. **Configure Alembic** to work with SQLAlchemy models. #### Frontend 1. **Set up Tailwind CSS** (if using): ```bash npx tailwindcss init -p ``` 2. **Configure Tailwind CSS** in `tailwind.config.js` and include it in your CSS files. ### Example `.env` File for Backend ```env DATABASE_URL=postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/ai_pm_tool JWT_SECRET=your_jwt_secret_key ``` ### Example `alembic.ini` Configuration Update the `sqlalchemy.url` in `alembic.ini` to match your database URL: ```ini sqlalchemy.url = postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/ai_pm_tool ``` ### Example `alembic/env.py` Configuration Update `env.py` to include your SQLAlchemy models: ```python from myapp.models import Base # Import your Base model target_metadata = Base.metadata ``` By installing these dependencies and setting up the environment, you'll have a solid foundation to start building the AI-driven project management tool. i am using dnd-kit instead of dnd-react. Also what is yup and formik Got it! If you're using `dnd-kit` instead of `react-dnd`, you can adjust the dependencies accordingly. Additionally, I'll explain what `Yup` and `Formik` are and how they can be useful in your project. ### Dependencies for Frontend (Next.js) with `dnd-kit` 1. **Next.js**: React framework for server-side rendering. 2. **React**: Library for building user interfaces. 3. **Axios**: For making HTTP requests to the backend. 4. **dnd-kit**: For drag-and-drop functionality in the Kanban board. 5. **Tailwind CSS**: For styling (optional, you can choose any CSS framework or plain CSS). 6. **Formik**: For form handling. 7. **Yup**: For form validation. ### Installation Commands #### Frontend (Next.js) First, create a new Next.js project if you haven't already: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest ai-pm-tool-frontend cd ai-pm-tool-frontend ``` Then, install the dependencies: ```bash npm install axios @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities tailwindcss formik yup ``` ### What are Formik and Yup? #### Formik Formik is a popular library for handling forms in React applications. It simplifies form state management, validation, and submission. Formik helps you manage form state, handle form submissions, and validate user inputs with ease. **Key Features of Formik:** - Simplifies form state management. - Provides easy-to-use hooks and components for form handling. - Integrates well with validation libraries like Yup. #### Yup Yup is a JavaScript schema builder for value parsing and validation. It is often used in conjunction with Formik to handle form validation. Yup allows you to define a schema for your form data and validate it against that schema. **Key Features of Yup:** - Schema-based validation. - Supports various data types and validation rules. - Integrates seamlessly with Formik for form validation. ### Example Usage of Formik and Yup Here's a simple example of how to use Formik and Yup together in a Next.js project: 1. **Install Formik and Yup**: ```bash npm install formik yup ``` 2. **Create a Form Component**: ```jsx // components/MyForm.js import React from 'react'; import { useFormik } from 'formik'; import * as Yup from 'yup'; const MyForm = () => { const formik = useFormik({ initialValues: { email: '', password: '', }, validationSchema: Yup.object({ email: Yup.string().email('Invalid email address').required('Required'), password: Yup.string().min(6, 'Password must be at least 6 characters').required('Required'), }), onSubmit: (values) => { console.log(values); }, }); return (
{formik.touched.email && formik.errors.email ? (
{formik.errors.email}
) : null}
{formik.touched.password && formik.errors.password ? (
{formik.errors.password}
) : null}
); }; export default MyForm; ``` 3. **Use the Form Component in a Page**: ```jsx // pages/index.js import React from 'react'; import MyForm from '../components/MyForm'; const Home = () => { return (

My Form

); }; export default Home; ``` ### Setting Up Tailwind CSS If you choose to use Tailwind CSS for styling, you can set it up as follows: 1. **Install Tailwind CSS**: ```bash npm install tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer npx tailwindcss init -p ``` 2. **Configure Tailwind CSS**: Update `tailwind.config.js`: ```js module.exports = { content: [ './pages/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}', './components/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}', ], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [], }; ``` 3. **Include Tailwind CSS in Your CSS File**: Update `styles/globals.css`: ```css @tailwind base; @tailwind components; @tailwind utilities; ``` By installing these dependencies and setting up the environment, you'll have a solid foundation to start building the AI-driven project management tool with the desired functionalities. Oh I wanted to use react hook form and zod Got it! If you prefer to use `react-hook-form` and `zod` for form handling and validation, that's a great choice. `react-hook-form` is a performant, flexible library for managing forms in React, and `zod` is a TypeScript-first schema declaration and validation library. ### Dependencies for Frontend (Next.js) with `react-hook-form` and `zod` 1. **Next.js**: React framework for server-side rendering. 2. **React**: Library for building user interfaces. 3. **Axios**: For making HTTP requests to the backend. 4. **dnd-kit**: For drag-and-drop functionality in the Kanban board. 5. **Tailwind CSS**: For styling (optional, you can choose any CSS framework or plain CSS). 6. **react-hook-form**: For form handling. 7. **zod**: For form validation. ### Installation Commands #### Frontend (Next.js) First, create a new Next.js project if you haven't already: ```bash npx create-next-app@latest ai-pm-tool-frontend cd ai-pm-tool-frontend ``` Then, install the dependencies: ```bash npm install axios @dnd-kit/core @dnd-kit/sortable @dnd-kit/utilities tailwindcss react-hook-form zod ``` ### Example Usage of `react-hook-form` and `zod` Here's a simple example of how to use `react-hook-form` and `zod` together in a Next.js project: 1. **Install `react-hook-form` and `zod`**: ```bash npm install react-hook-form zod ``` 2. **Create a Form Component**: ```jsx // components/MyForm.js import React from 'react'; import { useForm } from 'react-hook-form'; import { zodResolver } from '@hookform/resolvers/zod'; import * as z from 'zod'; const schema = z.object({ email: z.string().email('Invalid email address').nonempty('Required'), password: z.string().min(6, 'Password must be at least 6 characters').nonempty('Required'), }); const MyForm = () => { const { register, handleSubmit, formState: { errors }, } = useForm({ resolver: zodResolver(schema), }); const onSubmit = (data) => { console.log(data); }; return (
{errors.email &&
{errors.email.message}
}
{errors.password &&
{errors.password.message}
}
); }; export default MyForm; ``` 3. **Use the Form Component in a Page**: ```jsx // pages/index.js import React from 'react'; import MyForm from '../components/MyForm'; const Home = () => { return (

My Form

); }; export default Home; ``` ### Setting Up Tailwind CSS If you choose to use Tailwind CSS for styling, you can set it up as follows: 1. **Install Tailwind CSS**: ```bash npm install tailwindcss postcss autoprefixer npx tailwindcss init -p ``` 2. **Configure Tailwind CSS**: Update `tailwind.config.js`: ```js module.exports = { content: [ './pages/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}', './components/**/*.{js,ts,jsx,tsx}', ], theme: { extend: {}, }, plugins: [], }; ``` 3. **Include Tailwind CSS in Your CSS File**: Update `styles/globals.css`: ```css @tailwind base; @tailwind components; @tailwind utilities; ``` By installing these dependencies and setting up the environment, you'll have a solid foundation to start building the AI-driven project management tool with the desired functionalities. What is alembic for? Alembic is a lightweight database migration tool for use with SQLAlchemy, the Python SQL toolkit and Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) library. It allows you to manage database schema changes over time in a consistent and version-controlled manner. This is particularly useful in a collaborative environment where multiple developers might be working on the same project and making changes to the database schema. ### Key Features of Alembic 1. **Version Control for Database Schema**: Alembic allows you to track changes to your database schema over time, similar to how Git tracks changes to your codebase. 2. **Schema Migration**: It provides a way to apply and revert schema changes (migrations) to your database. 3. **Integration with SQLAlchemy**: Alembic is designed to work seamlessly with SQLAlchemy, making it easy to manage migrations for SQLAlchemy models. ### Why Use Alembic? 1. **Consistency**: Ensures that all developers and environments (development, staging, production) have the same database schema. 2. **Versioning**: Keeps track of different versions of the database schema, allowing you to upgrade or downgrade to specific versions as needed. 3. **Automation**: Automates the process of applying schema changes, reducing the risk of human error. ### Basic Workflow with Alembic 1. **Initialize Alembic**: Set up Alembic in your project. 2. **Create Migrations**: Generate migration scripts to reflect changes in your SQLAlchemy models. 3. **Apply Migrations**: Apply the migration scripts to update the database schema. ### Setting Up Alembic 1. **Install Alembic**: ```bash pip install alembic ``` 2. **Initialize Alembic**: In your project directory, run: ```bash alembic init alembic ``` This will create an `alembic` directory with configuration files and a `versions` directory to store migration scripts. 3. **Configure Alembic**: Update `alembic.ini` to set the database URL: ```ini # alembic.ini sqlalchemy.url = postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/ai_pm_tool ``` Update `env.py` to include your SQLAlchemy models: ```python # alembic/env.py from logging.config import fileConfig from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config from sqlalchemy import pool from alembic import context # Import your SQLAlchemy models here from myapp.models import Base # Replace with your actual models # this is the Alembic Config object, which provides # access to the values within the .ini file in use. config = context.config # Interpret the config file for Python logging. # This line sets up loggers basically. fileConfig(config.config_file_name) # add your model's MetaData object here # for 'autogenerate' support target_metadata = Base.metadata # other values from the config, defined by the needs of env.py, # can be acquired: # my_important_option = config.get_main_option("my_important_option") # ... etc. def run_migrations_offline(): """Run migrations in 'offline' mode. This configures the context with just a URL and not an Engine, though an Engine is acceptable here as well. By skipping the Engine creation we don't even need a DBAPI to be available. Calls to context.execute() here emit the given string to the script output. """ url = config.get_main_option("sqlalchemy.url") context.configure( url=url, target_metadata=target_metadata, literal_binds=True, dialect_opts={"paramstyle": "named"}, ) with context.begin_transaction(): context.run_migrations() def run_migrations_online(): """Run migrations in 'online' mode. In this scenario we need to create an Engine and associate a connection with the context. """ connectable = engine_from_config( config.get_section(config.config_ini_section), prefix="sqlalchemy.", poolclass=pool.NullPool, ) with connectable.connect() as connection: context.configure(connection=connection, target_metadata=target_metadata) with context.begin_transaction(): context.run_migrations() if context.is_offline_mode(): run_migrations_offline() else: run_migrations_online() ``` 4. **Create a Migration Script**: Whenever you make changes to your SQLAlchemy models, generate a new migration script: ```bash alembic revision --autogenerate -m "Add description to tasks" ``` This will create a new migration script in the `alembic/versions` directory. 5. **Apply Migrations**: Apply the migration to update the database schema: ```bash alembic upgrade head ``` This command will apply all pending migrations to bring the database schema up to date. ### Example SQLAlchemy Models and Alembic Configuration Here's an example of how you might set up your SQLAlchemy models and configure Alembic: 1. **SQLAlchemy Models**: ```python # myapp/models.py from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, ForeignKey, DateTime from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base import datetime Base = declarative_base() class User(Base): __tablename__ = 'users' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) email = Column(String, unique=True, nullable=False) password_hash = Column(String, nullable=False) role = Column(String, nullable=False) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) class Project(Base): __tablename__ = 'projects' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) name = Column(String, nullable=False) description = Column(String) owner_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('users.id')) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) owner = relationship('User', back_populates='projects') class Task(Base): __tablename__ = 'tasks' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) project_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('projects.id')) title = Column(String, nullable=False) description = Column(String) status = Column(String, default='To Do') assignee_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('users.id')) difficulty = Column(String) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) updated_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow, onupdate=datetime.datetime.utcnow) project = relationship('Project', back_populates='tasks') assignee = relationship('User', back_populates='tasks') User.projects = relationship('Project', order_by=Project.id, back_populates='owner') User.tasks = relationship('Task', order_by=Task.id, back_populates='assignee') Project.tasks = relationship('Task', order_by=Task.id, back_populates='project') ``` 2. **Alembic Configuration**: Update `env.py` to include your models: ```python # alembic/env.py from myapp.models import Base # Import your Base model target_metadata = Base.metadata ``` By using Alembic, you can manage your database schema changes in a structured and version-controlled manner, ensuring consistency across different environments and reducing the risk of errors. How do I set up my backend after I get all the needed dependencies. Is there a way to set up the file structure for the fast api easily? Could you go over that again. Sure! Setting up a well-structured backend with FastAPI involves organizing your files and directories in a way that makes your codebase maintainable and scalable. Here's a step-by-step guide to setting up your FastAPI backend, including the file structure and essential configurations. ### Step 1: Install Dependencies First, ensure you have all the necessary dependencies installed. If you haven't already, create a virtual environment and install the dependencies: ```bash python -m venv venv source venv/bin/activate # On Windows use `venv\Scripts\activate` pip install fastapi uvicorn sqlalchemy alembic pydantic bcrypt pyjwt httpx psycopg2-binary pip install transformers # If using Hugging Face Transformers for AI ``` ### Step 2: Set Up the Project Structure Here's a suggested file structure for your FastAPI project: ``` ai_pm_tool_backend/ ├── alembic/ │ ├── env.py │ ├── script.py.mako │ └── versions/ ├── app/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── main.py │ ├── config.py │ ├── models.py │ ├── schemas.py │ ├── crud.py │ ├── dependencies.py │ ├── routers/ │ │ ├── __init__.py │ │ ├── auth.py │ │ ├── projects.py │ │ ├── tasks.py │ └── services/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── ai.py │ ├── auth.py │ ├── load_balancer.py ├── .env ├── alembic.ini └── requirements.txt ``` ### Step 3: Configure Alembic 1. **Initialize Alembic**: ```bash alembic init alembic ``` 2. **Configure `alembic.ini`**: Update the `sqlalchemy.url` in `alembic.ini` to match your database URL: ```ini # alembic.ini sqlalchemy.url = postgresql://username:password@localhost:5432/ai_pm_tool ``` 3. **Update `env.py`**: Update `alembic/env.py` to include your SQLAlchemy models: ```python # alembic/env.py from logging.config import fileConfig from sqlalchemy import engine_from_config from sqlalchemy import pool from alembic import context # Import your SQLAlchemy models here from app.models import Base # Replace with your actual models # this is the Alembic Config object, which provides # access to the values within the .ini file in use. config = context.config # Interpret the config file for Python logging. # This line sets up loggers basically. fileConfig(config.config_file_name) # add your model's MetaData object here # for 'autogenerate' support target_metadata = Base.metadata # other values from the config, defined by the needs of env.py, # can be acquired: # my_important_option = config.get_main_option("my_important_option") # ... etc. def run_migrations_offline(): """Run migrations in 'offline' mode. This configures the context with just a URL and not an Engine, though an Engine is acceptable here as well. By skipping the Engine creation we don't even need a DBAPI to be available. Calls to context.execute() here emit the given string to the script output. """ url = config.get_main_option("sqlalchemy.url") context.configure( url=url, target_metadata=target_metadata, literal_binds=True, dialect_opts={"paramstyle": "named"}, ) with context.begin_transaction(): context.run_migrations() def run_migrations_online(): """Run migrations in 'online' mode. In this scenario we need to create an Engine and associate a connection with the context. """ connectable = engine_from_config( config.get_section(config.config_ini_section), prefix="sqlalchemy.", poolclass=pool.NullPool, ) with connectable.connect() as connection: context.configure(connection=connection, target_metadata=target_metadata) with context.begin_transaction(): context.run_migrations() if context.is_offline_mode(): run_migrations_offline() else: run_migrations_online() ``` ### Step 4: Create the FastAPI Application 1. **Create `app/main.py`**: ```python # app/main.py from fastapi import FastAPI from app.routers import auth, projects, tasks app = FastAPI() app.include_router(auth.router) app.include_router(projects.router) app.include_router(tasks.router) @app.get("/") def read_root(): return {"message": "Welcome to the AI-PM Tool API"} ``` 2. **Create `app/config.py`**: ```python # app/config.py from pydantic import BaseSettings class Settings(BaseSettings): database_url: str jwt_secret: str class Config: env_file = ".env" settings = Settings() ``` 3. **Create `app/models.py`**: ```python # app/models.py from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, ForeignKey, DateTime from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base import datetime Base = declarative_base() class User(Base): __tablename__ = 'users' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) email = Column(String, unique=True, nullable=False) password_hash = Column(String, nullable=False) role = Column(String, nullable=False) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) class Project(Base): __tablename__ = 'projects' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) name = Column(String, nullable=False) description = Column(String) owner_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('users.id')) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) owner = relationship('User', back_populates='projects') class Task(Base): __tablename__ = 'tasks' id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True) project_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('projects.id')) title = Column(String, nullable=False) description = Column(String) status = Column(String, default='To Do') assignee_id = Column(Integer, ForeignKey('users.id')) difficulty = Column(String) created_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow) updated_at = Column(DateTime, default=datetime.datetime.utcnow, onupdate=datetime.datetime.utcnow) project = relationship('Project', back_populates='tasks') assignee = relationship('User', back_populates='tasks') User.projects = relationship('Project', order_by=Project.id, back_populates='owner') User.tasks = relationship('Task', order_by=Task.id, back_populates='assignee') Project.tasks = relationship('Task', order_by=Task.id, back_populates='project') ``` 4. **Create `app/schemas.py`**: ```python # app/schemas.py from pydantic import BaseModel from typing import List, Optional from datetime import datetime class UserBase(BaseModel): email: str class UserCreate(UserBase): password: str class User(UserBase): id: int role: str created_at: datetime class Config: orm_mode = True class ProjectBase(BaseModel): name: str description: Optional[str] = None class ProjectCreate(ProjectBase): pass class Project(ProjectBase): id: int owner_id: int created_at: datetime class Config: orm_mode = True class TaskBase(BaseModel): title: str description: Optional[str] = None status: Optional[str] = "To Do" difficulty: Optional[str] = None class TaskCreate(TaskBase): pass class Task(TaskBase): id: int project_id: int assignee_id: Optional[int] = None created_at: datetime updated_at: datetime class Config: orm_mode = True ``` 5. **Create `app/crud.py`**: ```python # app/crud.py from sqlalchemy.orm import Session from app import models, schemas from app.dependencies import get_password_hash def get_user(db: Session, user_id: int): return db.query(models.User).filter(models.User.id == user_id).first() def get_user_by_email(db: Session, email: str): return db.query(models.User).filter(models.User.email == email).first() def create_user(db: Session, user: schemas.UserCreate): hashed_password = get_password_hash(user.password) db_user = models.User(email=user.email, password_hash=hashed_password, role="Regular User") db.add(db_user) db.commit() db.refresh(db_user) return db_user def get_projects(db: Session, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10): return db.query(models.Project).offset(skip).limit(limit).all() def create_project(db: Session, project: schemas.ProjectCreate, user_id: int): db_project = models.Project(**project.dict(), owner_id=user_id) db.add(db_project) db.commit() db.refresh(db_project) return db_project def get_tasks(db: Session, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10): return db.query(models.Task).offset(skip).limit(limit).all() def create_task(db: Session, task: schemas.TaskCreate, project_id: int): db_task = models.Task(**task.dict(), project_id=project_id) db.add(db_task) db.commit() db.refresh(db_task) return db_task ``` 6. **Create `app/dependencies.py`**: ```python # app/dependencies.py from passlib.context import CryptContext from jose import JWTError, jwt from datetime import datetime, timedelta from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, status from fastapi.security import OAuth2PasswordBearer from sqlalchemy.orm import Session from app import models, schemas from app.database import SessionLocal from app.config import settings pwd_context = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"], deprecated="auto") oauth2_scheme = OAuth2PasswordBearer(tokenUrl="token") SECRET_KEY = settings.jwt_secret ALGORITHM = "HS256" ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES = 30 def get_password_hash(password): return pwd_context.hash(password) def verify_password(plain_password, hashed_password): return pwd_context.verify(plain_password, hashed_password) def create_access_token(data: dict, expires_delta: timedelta = None): to_encode = data.copy() if expires_delta: expire = datetime.utcnow() + expires_delta else: expire = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(minutes=15) to_encode.update({"exp": expire}) encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(to_encode, SECRET_KEY, algorithm=ALGORITHM) return encoded_jwt def get_db(): db = SessionLocal() try: yield db finally: db.close() def get_current_user(db: Session = Depends(get_db), token: str = Depends(oauth2_scheme)): credentials_exception = HTTPException( status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="Could not validate credentials", headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"}, ) try: payload = jwt.decode(token, SECRET_KEY, algorithms=[ALGORITHM]) user_id: str = payload.get("sub") if user_id is None: raise credentials_exception except JWTError: raise credentials_exception user = db.query(models.User).filter(models.User.id == user_id).first() if user is None: raise credentials_exception return user ``` 7. **Create `app/routers/auth.py`**: ```python # app/routers/auth.py from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException, status from sqlalchemy.orm import Session from app import schemas, crud from app.dependencies import get_db, create_access_token, verify_password router = APIRouter() @router.post("/register", response_model=schemas.User) def register(user: schemas.UserCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db)): db_user = crud.get_user_by_email(db, email=user.email) if db_user: raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail="Email already registered") return crud.create_user(db=db, user=user) @router.post("/token", response_model=schemas.Token) def login_for_access_token(form_data: schemas.LoginForm, db: Session = Depends(get_db)): user = crud.get_user_by_email(db, email=form_data.username) if not user or not verify_password(form_data.password, user.password_hash): raise HTTPException( status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED, detail="Incorrect username or password", headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"}, ) access_token = create_access_token(data={"sub": str(user.id)}) return {"access_token": access_token, "token_type": "bearer"} ``` 8. **Create `app/routers/projects.py`**: ```python # app/routers/projects.py from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException from sqlalchemy.orm import Session from app import schemas, crud from app.dependencies import get_db, get_current_user router = APIRouter() @router.get("/projects", response_model=List[schemas.Project]) def read_projects(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10, db: Session = Depends(get_db)): projects = crud.get_projects(db, skip=skip, limit=limit) return projects @router.post("/projects", response_model=schemas.Project) def create_project(project: schemas.ProjectCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db), current_user: schemas.User = Depends(get_current_user)): return crud.create_project(db=db, project=project, user_id=current_user.id) ``` 9. **Create `app/routers/tasks.py`**: ```python # app/routers/tasks.py from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException from sqlalchemy.orm import Session from app import schemas, crud from app.dependencies import get_db, get_current_user router = APIRouter() @router.get("/tasks", response_model=List[schemas.Task]) def read_tasks(skip: int = 0, limit: int = 10, db: Session = Depends(get_db)): tasks = crud.get_tasks(db, skip=skip, limit=limit) return tasks @router.post("/tasks", response_model=schemas.Task) def create_task(task: schemas.TaskCreate, db: Session = Depends(get_db), current_user: schemas.User = Depends(get_current_user)): return crud.create_task(db=db, task=task, project_id=current_user.id) ``` ### Step 5: Run the Application 1. **Create the Database**: ```bash alembic revision --autogenerate -m "Initial migration" alembic upgrade head ``` 2. **Run the FastAPI Application**: ```bash uvicorn app.main:app --reload ``` Your FastAPI application should now be running, and you can start building out the rest of the functionality as described in your project requirements. This setup provides a solid foundation for your AI-driven project management tool. Do I have to put something in the __init__.py files? The `__init__.py` files are used to mark directories on disk as Python package directories. In many cases, these files can be empty, but they can also be used to initialize package-level variables, import submodules, or define package-level functions and classes. Here's a brief overview of what you might include in the `__init__.py` files for your FastAPI project: ### `app/__init__.py` This file can be empty, but you might include package-level imports or initializations if needed. ```python # app/__init__.py # This file can be empty or include package-level imports/initializations ``` ### `app/routers/__init__.py` This file can be used to import all the routers so they can be easily included in the main application. ```python # app/routers/__init__.py from .auth import router as auth_router from .projects import router as projects_router from .tasks import router as tasks_router ``` ### `app/services/__init__.py` This file can be empty or include package-level imports/initializations. ```python # app/services/__init__.py # This file can be empty or include package-level imports/initializations ``` ### Example of a Complete Project Structure Here's how your project structure might look with the `__init__.py` files: ``` ai_pm_tool_backend/ ├── alembic/ │ ├── env.py │ ├── script.py.mako │ └── versions/ ├── app/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── main.py │ ├── config.py │ ├── models.py │ ├── schemas.py │ ├── crud.py │ ├── dependencies.py │ ├── routers/ │ │ ├── __init__.py │ │ ├── auth.py │ │ ├── projects.py │ │ ├── tasks.py │ └── services/ │ ├── __init__.py │ ├── ai.py │ ├── auth.py │ ├── load_balancer.py ├── .env ├── alembic.ini └── requirements.txt ``` ### Example Content for `__init__.py` Files #### `app/__init__.py` ```python # app/__init__.py # This file can be empty or include package-level imports/initializations ``` #### `app/routers/__init__.py` ```python # app/routers/__init__.py from .auth import router as auth_router from .projects import router as projects_router from .tasks import router as tasks_router ``` #### `app/services/__init__.py` ```python # app/services/__init__.py # This file can be empty or include package-level imports/initializations ``` ### Using the Imports in `main.py` You can then use the imports from `app/routers/__init__.py` in your `main.py` to include the routers: ```python # app/main.py from fastapi import FastAPI from app.routers import auth_router, projects_router, tasks_router app = FastAPI() app.include_router(auth_router) app.include_router(projects_router) app.include_router(tasks_router) @app.get("/") def read_root(): return {"message": "Welcome to the AI-PM Tool API"} ``` By organizing your project in this way, you keep your code modular and maintainable, making it easier to manage and scale as your project grows.
